Q: Used limited free prep mostly to diagnose that Quant timing was the main risk.
Drilled free official Quant questions, GMAT Club questions, and Magoosh trial questions to refresh old math skills.
Used explanation videos for missed Quant questions to learn targeted tricks, especially for combinatorics and polynomials.
Made answering nearly all Quant questions the top test-day priority after leaving many blank in practice.
Applied a 2.5-minute threshold: if a question still felt far from solved, guessed and moved on.
Used a short attempt to intuit the best guess instead of blindly selecting when time was up.
V: Relied on a strong reading and humanities background for Verbal.
Flagged uncertain Verbal questions for review and changed only when there was a clear reason.
Kept Verbal last because it was the easiest section and fatigue was less likely to damage performance there.
DI: Benefited from work experience involving graphs and data visualization.
Practiced some DI without a calculator to improve fast arithmetic.
Used the same time-awareness lesson from Quant after nearly running out of time on DI.
overall: Used a low-investment three-week attempt to establish whether a high score was possible before paying for extensive prep.
Took official practice tests and free starter-kit materials to identify weak timing and Quant gaps.
Chose section order Quant, DI, then Verbal from hardest to easiest because restlessness would grow later.
Stayed relaxed on test day after a poor final mock and focused on execution rather than prediction.
Would use official question banks and a prep service for a retake focused on Quant.

Q: Revised mathematical concepts and summary PDFs regularly to rebuild a rusty Quant base.
Used quizzes and regular revision to compensate for being years out of engineering coursework.
Avoided overinvesting in the first few questions because early extra time could cause larger penalties later.
Learned not to interpret easy questions as proof that the adaptive algorithm was going badly.
V: Prepared Verbal despite not being an avid reader by relying on repeated practice and pattern familiarity.
For hard RC passages, avoided rereading endlessly; moved to questions and searched the passage for what was needed.
Used many mocks to become familiar with likely Verbal patterns before the official test.
DI: Rejected the misconception that Quant and Verbal prep alone would fully cover DI.
After the first attempt, practiced all four DI subsections in a focused manner.
Used GMAT Club forum quizzes and e-GMAT Scholaranium for DI sub-sectional and sectional practice.
Improved DI by overcoming the belief that an 82 ceiling was fixed.
Took longer deep breaths before DI on the second attempt to stay calm.
overall: Prepared consistently with a personalized study plan and five to six hours of daily work.
Analyzed every question in detail, treating review as part of the quiz itself despite not keeping a formal error log.
Took many mocks and followed the exam-day schedule for the final two weeks so the real exam felt like another mock.
After each mock, reviewed missed questions before checking answers and then practiced a few weak-area questions.
Focused on adapting to the situation, bookmarking difficult questions, and returning with a calmer mind.

Q: Spent significant time on each Quant topic the first time through to internalize strategies, shortcuts, and traps.
Compared alternate solution approaches and adopted new strategies only when they had clear advantages.
Delayed timed Quant practice until after learning the main techniques and traps.
Timed full 21-question Quant sections to preserve focus and avoid overconfidence.
Recognized that silly mistakes can arise after overpreparation makes Quant feel too easy.
V: Studied CR and RC separately rather than treating Verbal as one skill.
For CR, absorbed the logic in explanations and used elimination for close choices.
Used red flags such as extreme words, extreme inferences, and overgeneralization to eliminate CR traps.
For RC, emphasized GMAT-style passage practice over general reading.
Practiced all available RC passages from GMAT Club, TOP One Percent, and official guides, with LSAT RC as an extension source.
DI: Treated DI as a mix of Quant and Verbal and waited until both foundations were built before emphasizing it.
Made Data Sufficiency fast and efficient so it could save time in DI.
Improved DI timing through repeated practice, especially for MSR, tables, and charts.
Used a Quant-to-DI transition to preserve analytical flow before moving to Verbal.
overall: Used TOP One Percent, GMAT Club Elite tests, and GMAT Club community resources for preparation and doubt resolution.
Regularly attempted Butler questions, Bunuel collections, Bismuth DI collections, and forum explanations.
Initially planned Quant, Verbal, break, DI, but changed to Quant, DI, break, Verbal after practice tests showed fatigue before DI.
On test day, skipped the planned break because the first two sections left him in a strong flow state.
Later analyzed mistakes through self-review to understand what went wrong and what worked.
